IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem (historical text normalization) and demonstrates a consistent, though modest, improvement (~2% absolute, up to ~3%) from a well-motivated MTL G2P auxiliary task across a broad set of 44 Anselm texts using CELEX. From an experimental-methods perspective the contribution is useful—it shows that phonetic auxiliary supervision can complement attention and resolves partly distinct errors—but the gains are incremental, depend on limited per-text training data and a single manuscript for tuning, and models operate on isolated wordforms (no context). Overall this is a solid, reproducible direction likely to be built upon by practitioners in the subfield, but not transformative for the broader NLP community. | B(3): The paper tackles a practically important task for humanities NLP—historical text normalization—and demonstrates consistent, if modest, gains (≈2% absolute, up to ~3%) across a diverse set of 44 Early New High German texts by using G2P as an auxiliary MTL signal. From my computational-historical perspective, showing that phonology-driven MTL can induce attention-like behavior and resolve complementary error types is insightful and opens realistic follow-ups (cross-linguistic phonology auxiliaries, context-aware normalization). However, reliance on CELEX, per-text small training sets, and evaluation on a single language/period limit immediate broad adoption; the contribution is solid and useful but not transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem (historical text normalization) and demonstrates a consistent, though modest, improvement (~2% absolute, up to ~3%) from a well-motivated MTL G2P auxiliary task across a broad set of 44 Anselm texts using CELEX. From an experimental-methods perspective the contribution is useful—it shows that phonetic auxiliary supervision can complement attention and resolves partly distinct errors—but the gains are incremental, depend on limited per-text training data and a single manuscript for tuning, and models operate on isolated wordforms (no context). Overall this is a solid, reproducible direction likely to be built upon by practitioners in the subfield, but not transformative for the broader NLP community.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a practically important task for humanities NLP—historical text normalization—and demonstrates consistent, if modest, gains (≈2% absolute, up to ~3%) across a diverse set of 44 Early New High German texts by using G2P as an auxiliary MTL signal. From my computational-historical perspective, showing that phonology-driven MTL can induce attention-like behavior and resolve complementary error types is insightful and opens realistic follow-ups (cross-linguistic phonology auxiliaries, context-aware normalization). However, reliance on CELEX, per-text small training sets, and evaluation on a single language/period limit immediate broad adoption; the contribution is solid and useful but not transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a topical NLP problem (historical text normalization) with methods (seq2seq with attention, multi-task learning using CELEX G2P) and evaluations (44-text Anselm corpus, comparisons to state-of-the-art baselines, word accuracy/error analyses) that match expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. The experimental design is largely appropriate and audience-relevant, but there are some reproducibility and robustness concerns (per-text training sets are small, hyperparameters tuned on a single held-out manuscript, and code release is not mentioned) that prevent a perfect fit without minor clarifications or additional controls. | B(4): This paper fits well within a top NLP venue: it addresses a recognized task in historical text normalization using contemporary seq2seq and multi-task learning techniques, evaluates across a large and diverse set of 44 Early New High German texts, and reports standard metrics and comparisons to strong baselines (perceptron aligner, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma). The use of a G2P auxiliary task leveraging CELEX is linguistically motivated and of clear interest to the computational-historical-linguistics community, though the per-text small training sizes, lack of contextual token modeling, and reliance on CELEX limit broader ML novelty and generality. Given these strengths and limitations, the paper is a good fit for a top NLP conference (slightly less so for a general ML venue).

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a topical NLP problem (historical text normalization) with methods (seq2seq with attention, multi-task learning using CELEX G2P) and evaluations (44-text Anselm corpus, comparisons to state-of-the-art baselines, word accuracy/error analyses) that match expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. The experimental design is largely appropriate and audience-relevant, but there are some reproducibility and robustness concerns (per-text training sets are small, hyperparameters tuned on a single held-out manuscript, and code release is not mentioned) that prevent a perfect fit without minor clarifications or additional controls.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits well within a top NLP venue: it addresses a recognized task in historical text normalization using contemporary seq2seq and multi-task learning techniques, evaluates across a large and diverse set of 44 Early New High German texts, and reports standard metrics and comparisons to strong baselines (perceptron aligner, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma). The use of a G2P auxiliary task leveraging CELEX is linguistically motivated and of clear interest to the computational-historical-linguistics community, though the per-text small training sizes, lack of contextual token modeling, and reliance on CELEX limit broader ML novelty and generality. Given these strengths and limitations, the paper is a good fit for a top NLP conference (slightly less so for a general ML venue).

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies established sequence-to-sequence attention and multi-task learning (G2P as an auxiliary task using CELEX) to historical text normalization and demonstrates modest empirical gains; this is a coherent and useful engineering contribution but not a radical new method. The claim that MTL can induce attention-like behavior and the cross-text evaluation on 44 Anselm manuscripts add empirical novelty, yet the core techniques (encoder–decoder, soft attention, MTL with grapheme-to-phoneme supervision) are known. From an experimental-methods perspective the work is an incremental, well-documented extension rather than a fundamentally new idea that would surprise experts. | B(3): The paper applies established sequence-to-sequence and attention architectures plus multi-task learning with a G2P auxiliary to historical normalization—an intuitively sensible and practically useful combination but not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Its use of CELEX phonetic transcriptions and the demonstration across 44 Anselm texts add empirical breadth, and the analysis showing overlap between attention and MTL behaviors is interesting, yet the core methods (encoder–decoder LSTMs, attention, beam search, MTL) are well-known in related NLP tasks. From the perspective of historical NLP, this is a solid, moderate-novelty contribution (non-trivial engineering and evaluation) rather than a breakthrough methodological advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies established sequence-to-sequence attention and multi-task learning (G2P as an auxiliary task using CELEX) to historical text normalization and demonstrates modest empirical gains; this is a coherent and useful engineering contribution but not a radical new method. The claim that MTL can induce attention-like behavior and the cross-text evaluation on 44 Anselm manuscripts add empirical novelty, yet the core techniques (encoder–decoder, soft attention, MTL with grapheme-to-phoneme supervision) are known. From an experimental-methods perspective the work is an incremental, well-documented extension rather than a fundamentally new idea that would surprise experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies established sequence-to-sequence and attention architectures plus multi-task learning with a G2P auxiliary to historical normalization—an intuitively sensible and practically useful combination but not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Its use of CELEX phonetic transcriptions and the demonstration across 44 Anselm texts add empirical breadth, and the analysis showing overlap between attention and MTL behaviors is interesting, yet the core methods (encoder–decoder LSTMs, attention, beam search, MTL) are well-known in related NLP tasks. From the perspective of historical NLP, this is a solid, moderate-novelty contribution (non-trivial engineering and evaluation) rather than a breakthrough methodological advance.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (MTL encoder–decoder with G2P auxiliary task improving normalization). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters, datasets, decoding, and lexicon filtering) are reported, and figures/tables/examples are described as informative. Minor clarity issues remain: the treatment of filtered training samples versus evaluation examples is potentially confusing, and code availability is not mentioned, which would help reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusions, and its main contributions (MTL with G2P auxiliary task and attention comparisons) are stated early. Notation for the encoder–decoder and training setup is consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples (e.g., ambiguity cases, error counts) effectively illustrate results across the 44 Anselm texts. A few presentation gaps remain: reliance on a single manuscript for hyperparameter tuning and the treatment of filtered long samples could be highlighted more clearly, and explicit mention of code availability is missing. Overall the writing is good and largely unambiguous, with only minor clarity issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (MTL encoder–decoder with G2P auxiliary task improving normalization). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters, datasets, decoding, and lexicon filtering) are reported, and figures/tables/examples are described as informative. Minor clarity issues remain: the treatment of filtered training samples versus evaluation examples is potentially confusing, and code availability is not mentioned, which would help reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusions, and its main contributions (MTL with G2P auxiliary task and attention comparisons) are stated early. Notation for the encoder–decoder and training setup is consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples (e.g., ambiguity cases, error counts) effectively illustrate results across the 44 Anselm texts. A few presentation gaps remain: reliance on a single manuscript for hyperparameter tuning and the treatment of filtered long samples could be highlighted more clearly, and explicit mention of code availability is missing. Overall the writing is good and largely unambiguous, with only minor clarity issues.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents clear main claims and a reasonably broad empirical evaluation across 44 Anselm texts showing ~2% absolute gains for the proposed MTL architectures, and it compares to relevant baselines (perceptron, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma). However, experimental methodology has gaps: hyperparameters were tuned on a single manuscript (potential overfitting), training filtered long samples while evaluation did not, and no mention of statistical significance or multiple-seed runs to assess stability. These issues create some concerns about the robustness of the reported gains and whether all claims (e.g., MTL inducing attention-like behavior) are fully supported by controlled ablations and statistical evidence. | B(4): The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across a reasonably large and diverse set of 44 Anselm texts, showing consistent ~2% absolute gains and detailed error analyses comparing attention and MTL variants. The methodology uses sensible baselines (perceptron+Levenshtein, state-of-the-art sequence-labeler, Norma), reports hyperparameters and evaluation metrics, and analyses learned behavior; however, there are some methodological weaknesses (hyperparameters tuned on a single held-out manuscript, training-data filtering of long samples while keeping them in test, and no mention of statistical significance testing) that could affect the robustness of the reported improvements. There are no mathematical derivations to check and no evident logical contradictions in the experimental claims, so overall the results are technically credible though a few experimental-controls are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear main claims and a reasonably broad empirical evaluation across 44 Anselm texts showing ~2% absolute gains for the proposed MTL architectures, and it compares to relevant baselines (perceptron, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma). However, experimental methodology has gaps: hyperparameters were tuned on a single manuscript (potential overfitting), training filtered long samples while evaluation did not, and no mention of statistical significance or multiple-seed runs to assess stability. These issues create some concerns about the robustness of the reported gains and whether all claims (e.g., MTL inducing attention-like behavior) are fully supported by controlled ablations and statistical evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across a reasonably large and diverse set of 44 Anselm texts, showing consistent ~2% absolute gains and detailed error analyses comparing attention and MTL variants. The methodology uses sensible baselines (perceptron+Levenshtein, state-of-the-art sequence-labeler, Norma), reports hyperparameters and evaluation metrics, and analyses learned behavior; however, there are some methodological weaknesses (hyperparameters tuned on a single held-out manuscript, training-data filtering of long samples while keeping them in test, and no mention of statistical significance testing) that could affect the robustness of the reported improvements. There are no mathematical derivations to check and no evident logical contradictions in the experimental claims, so overall the results are technically credible though a few experimental-controls are missing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Wieling et al. 2009, Bollmann & Søgaard 2016, and the Norma tool) and evaluates on the same Anselm texts with the same metrics (word accuracy / error counts), reporting direct gains vs prior state-of-the-art. Comparisons appear generally fair, though hyperparameters were tuned on a single held-out manuscript (B) which may bias reported averages and no statistical testing or ablation significance is reported. The authors acknowledge cases where prior methods are competitive and analyze error overlap between attention and MTL, but more controlled sensitivity analyses (e.g., decoding/lexicon-filtering effects, seeds) and explicit release of code/conditions would strengthen the fairness claim. | B(4): The paper compares to the established baselines for this task (Wieling et al. 2009 perceptron+Levenshtein, Bollmann & Søgaard 2016 bi-LSTM tagger in both standard and MTL variants, and the Norma tool), using the same Anselm corpus and word-accuracy metrics, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors report when prior methods are competitive and quantify where their MTL/attention models yield modest but consistent gains (~2–3%), and they analyze error overlaps between attention and MTL. Minor omissions remain — for example, direct comparison to other contemporary neural encoder–decoder normalization efforts or broader cross-text generalization experiments would strengthen claims of novelty and robustness — hence not a fully comprehensive comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Wieling et al. 2009, Bollmann & Søgaard 2016, and the Norma tool) and evaluates on the same Anselm texts with the same metrics (word accuracy / error counts), reporting direct gains vs prior state-of-the-art. Comparisons appear generally fair, though hyperparameters were tuned on a single held-out manuscript (B) which may bias reported averages and no statistical testing or ablation significance is reported. The authors acknowledge cases where prior methods are competitive and analyze error overlap between attention and MTL, but more controlled sensitivity analyses (e.g., decoding/lexicon-filtering effects, seeds) and explicit release of code/conditions would strengthen the fairness claim.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to the established baselines for this task (Wieling et al. 2009 perceptron+Levenshtein, Bollmann & Søgaard 2016 bi-LSTM tagger in both standard and MTL variants, and the Norma tool), using the same Anselm corpus and word-accuracy metrics, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors report when prior methods are competitive and quantify where their MTL/attention models yield modest but consistent gains (~2–3%), and they analyze error overlaps between attention and MTL. Minor omissions remain — for example, direct comparison to other contemporary neural encoder–decoder normalization efforts or broader cross-text generalization experiments would strengthen claims of novelty and robustness — hence not a fully comprehensive comparison.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation across 44 texts, comparisons to relevant baselines (perceptron, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma), and multiple architectures (attention, MTL, combined) support the central claims. The paper includes some ablations (attention vs MTL and their combination) and discusses limitations (small per-text training sets, context-free input, filtering of long samples), but it lacks important reproducibility controls and sensitivity analyses—no code/seeds mentioned, tuning was done on a single manuscript (B) which risks overfitting, and I see no statistical significance testing or decoding/lexicon-sensitivity analyses. Related work coverage is adequate. Overall the study is solid but would benefit from additional controlled ablations, significance testing, and full release of code/data splits to be fully convincing. | B(4): The paper runs experiments across a large and diverse set of 44 Anselm texts, compares against strong baselines, and systematically evaluates key design choices (attention vs non-attention, MTL auxiliary G2P, and combined models), including analyses of error overlap and learned parameter behavior. It discusses realistic limitations (small per-text training sets, token-isolation ambiguity, training-data filtering) and reports hyperparameters, but sensitivity analyses are limited (hyperparameters tuned on a single manuscript and no broad robustness checks across tuning choices) and code availability is not stated, which hampers reproducibility and broader adoption given the CELEX dependency. Overall the empirical evaluation is thorough and supports the claims, with minor gaps in robustness/sensitivity and release of artifacts.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation across 44 texts, comparisons to relevant baselines (perceptron, bi-LSTM tagger, Norma), and multiple architectures (attention, MTL, combined) support the central claims. The paper includes some ablations (attention vs MTL and their combination) and discusses limitations (small per-text training sets, context-free input, filtering of long samples), but it lacks important reproducibility controls and sensitivity analyses—no code/seeds mentioned, tuning was done on a single manuscript (B) which risks overfitting, and I see no statistical significance testing or decoding/lexicon-sensitivity analyses. Related work coverage is adequate. Overall the study is solid but would benefit from additional controlled ablations, significance testing, and full release of code/data splits to be fully convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs experiments across a large and diverse set of 44 Anselm texts, compares against strong baselines, and systematically evaluates key design choices (attention vs non-attention, MTL auxiliary G2P, and combined models), including analyses of error overlap and learned parameter behavior. It discusses realistic limitations (small per-text training sets, token-isolation ambiguity, training-data filtering) and reports hyperparameters, but sensitivity analyses are limited (hyperparameters tuned on a single manuscript and no broad robustness checks across tuning choices) and code availability is not stated, which hampers reproducibility and broader adoption given the CELEX dependency. Overall the empirical evaluation is thorough and supports the claims, with minor gaps in robustness/sensitivity and release of artifacts.

main_claims: Introducing a multi-task learning (MTL) encoder-decoder architecture that uses grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) prediction as an auxiliary task improves historical text normalization.

The proposed neural architectures (with attention and/or MTL) are competitive with and improve upon prior state-of-the-art methods, yielding roughly a 2% absolute gain (and up to almost 3% for the best architecture).
Multi-task learning can induce behavior similar to an explicit attention mechanism; attention and MTL resolve overlapping but partly distinct errors and show similar learned parameter changes.
The approach is evaluated across a diverse set of 44 Early New High German texts from the Anselm corpus and benefits from using CELEX phonetic transcriptions as auxiliary data.
_summary.methodology: Train character-level encoder–decoder LSTM sequence-to-sequence models (optionally with soft attention) to map historical wordforms to modern normalizations, and augment with multi-task learning that alternates training on a G2P auxiliary task using CELEX; use Adam optimization, greedy or beam decoding (beam size 5), and optional lexicon filtering.
datasets: Anselm corpus (Early New High German) — 44 texts (training/eval split per text)
CELEX lexical database (German part) — phonetic transcriptions for G2P auxiliary task
baselines: Averaged perceptron model with Levenshtein-based alignment (Wieling et al., 2009)
Deep bi-LSTM sequential tagger (Bollmann and Søgaard, 2016) — evaluated in standard and MTL variants
Norma tool (rule-based and Levenshtein-based algorithms, Bollmann 2012)
Sequence-labeling approach by Bollmann and Søgaard cited as state-of-the-art comparison
metrics: Word error counts (average number of word errors resolved/introduced)
limitations: Training data is limited (per-text training sets range ~2,000–11,000 tokens; overall texts are relatively short) and hyperparameters are tuned on a single manuscript (B) left out of reported averages.
Models consider word forms in isolation (no token context), which is problematic for ambiguous cases (example: 'jn' ambiguous between 'in' and 'ihn').
During training, samples with input or output words longer than 14 characters are filtered out (172 samples) — though evaluation includes all test examples.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - historical text normalization / character-level neural sequence-to-sequence models

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in NLP, extensive experience building and evaluating sequence-to-sequence models and productionizing research code for reproducible benchmarks.
traits: Insists on rigorous experimental design: clear baselines, controlled ablations, and statistical testing for claimed gains
Pays close attention to dataset splits, preprocessing, and potential data leakage or implicit supervision
Values complete reporting of hyperparameters, optimization details, and training/validation curves to assess stability and reproducibility
Checks decoding and evaluation choices (greedy vs beam, lexicon filtering) and their impact via sensitivity analyses
Looks for artifacts release (code, seeds, data versions) and clear instructions to replicate results
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Systematic, evidence-focused reviewer who highlights missing controls and requests targeted experiments or clarity to validate empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Historical NLP / Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in computational historical linguistics and text normalization with a track record of applying NLP methods to diachronic corpora and resources for low-resource orthographies.
traits: Evaluates novelty relative to prior work in normalization, G2P, and multi-task learning, and assesses theoretical motivation
Considers the linguistic plausibility and interpretability of model behaviors (e.g., whether phonology-based auxiliaries make sense for the language and epoch)
Examines corpus selection and diversity (representativeness of the 44 texts, domain shifts) and practical implications for downstream humanities research
Values discussion of limitations, generalizability to other languages/periods, and whether gains translate into meaningful real-world impact
Notices how the approach integrates with existing pipelines (lexicon filtering, CELEX reliance) and whether dependencies limit broader adoption
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-aware, impact-oriented reviewer who balances appreciation of practical gains with scrutiny of novelty, interpretability, and broader applicability.
